content.text: Mob programming—also called ensemble programming—brings the entire team to a single keyboard and screen to solve problems together. In agentic AI development, where output is non-deterministic and reasoning chains are opaque, the mob format provides a natural countermeasure: multiple observers catch LLM misbehavior, debate prompt choices in real time, and build shared understanding of the agent system's behaviour.

attrs.alt: Mob Programming role rotation around a central Agent System, showing Driver, Navigator, Prompt Tester, Researcher, and Mob Observer roles with rotation arrows

content.text: Mob programming is a software development practice where the whole team works on the same thing, at the same time, in the same space, on the same computer. One person is the
(observe, research, suggest). Roles rotate on a fixed timer—typically 10 to 15 minutes. In AI and agent feature work, the mob gains two additional specialist roles: a
who continuously exercises the agent with sample inputs during the session, and a
who looks up model documentation, API constraints, or prior art.

Agent systems exhibit behaviour that no single developer can fully observe or predict. A prompt that works perfectly on one input can fail silently on a structurally similar input. LLM outputs vary across runs, model versions, and temperature settings. These properties make solo development risky: the author of a prompt is the person least likely to discover its failure modes. Mob programming addresses this by ensuring multiple perspectives witness every output and challenge every assumption about agent behaviour as the code is written.

content: nobody is the sole author of a critical system prompt, reducing key-person risk.
five observers catch regressions that a solo developer would miss in a quick test.
the whole team understands the agent's tool-use graph and context management after a mob session.
the Prompt Tester role means prompts are exercised continuously, not only in CI.
junior developers learn prompt engineering and agent design by watching and participating.
code reviewed by the mob in real time requires lighter async review afterward.
five developers for four hours is twenty developer-hours for a single session.
maintaining focus while not driving is difficult; strict role rotation mitigates this.
remote mobs need low-latency screen-share and a shared terminal, which adds friction.
routine refactors and non-AI code are cheaper to pair on or solo.

content.text: A mob session follows a structured loop: roles rotate on a timer, the Driver types only what the Navigator dictates, and the Navigator synthesises input from the mob. For agent feature work, the Prompt Tester runs sample interactions in a second terminal window throughout the session. The Researcher maintains a live doc of reference materials—model documentation, internal runbooks, past incident notes.
Backlog item scoping (15 min before session):

content: the mob agrees on a single, completable goal for the session. For agent work this means a specific capability or a specific prompt to write and test.
shared screen started, environment confirmed working, sample inputs loaded in the Prompt Tester's terminal.
timer starts, Driver types only what Navigator says, Navigator gets input from the mob.
Driver → Mob, Navigator → Driver, Mob Member → Navigator or Prompt Tester.
what did the agent do that surprised us? What prompt decision would we make differently?

content.text: The mob session operates around a single shared workstation or screen. The Driver controls the IDE; the Prompt Tester controls a second terminal running the agent against live or mock inputs; the Researcher has a browser and reference documents. Everything visible to one person is visible to all.
Types code and prompt text—implements what the Navigator dictates
Thinks at the highest level of abstraction—directs the Driver without touching the keyboard
Continuously runs the agent against sample inputs and reports anomalies to the mob
Looks up API docs, model capabilities, past prompt experiments, and relevant papers
Watches both the code and the agent output, raises concerns, suggests alternatives
Designing a new agent's initial system prompt and tool schema.
Debugging a non-deterministic agent failure that one developer cannot reproduce reliably.
Writing the first version of a retrieval-augmented prompt where the context window budget is constrained.
Onboarding new team members to an existing multi-agent system.
Post-incident prompt remediation where the team needs to agree on a fix.
Evaluation rubric design, where multiple perspectives are needed to define what 'correct' output looks like.

Adopting mob programming for agent work requires three things: a physical or virtual shared environment, role rotation discipline, and a session log. The session log is especially important for agent work because it records what inputs the Prompt Tester used and what anomalies the mob observed.

# mob-config.yaml — configuration for mob session tooling (mob.sh or similar)
mob:
  timer_minutes: 12
  roles:
    - name: Driver
      description: Types only what Navigator says; controls the IDE
      rotation: every_cycle
    - name: Navigator
      description: Thinks one level above the code; synthesises mob input
      rotation: every_cycle
    - name: Prompt Tester
      description: Runs agent against sample inputs in a second terminal
      rotation: every_other_cycle
    - name: Researcher
      description: Looks up docs, prior prompts, model changelogs
      rotation: every_other_cycle

  session:
    goal_template: "Write and validate the prompt for: {capability}"
    checkin_interval_minutes: 45
    retro_duration_minutes: 10

  tooling:
    screen_share: "tuple"      # or: pop, zoom, vscode-liveshare
    shared_terminal: true
    session_log: true
    log_path: "mob-logs/{date}-{goal-slug}.md"

# mob_session_log.py — structured logger for mob sessions on agent features
from dataclasses import dataclass, field, asdict
from datetime import datetime, timezone
from typing import Optional
import json
import pathlib


@dataclass
class PromptObservation:
    input_text: str
    output_text: str
    observed_at: str
    observer: str
    anomaly: Optional[str] = None


@dataclass
class MobCycle:
    cycle_number: int
    driver: str
    navigator: str
    prompt_tester: str
    researcher: str
    started_at: str
    ended_at: Optional[str] = None
    decisions: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)
    observations: list[PromptObservation] = field(default_factory=list)

    def add_decision(self, decision: str) -> None:
        self.decisions.append(decision)

    def add_observation(self, obs: PromptObservation) -> None:
        self.observations.append(obs)

    def close(self) -> None:
        self.ended_at = datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat()


@dataclass
class MobSession:
    session_id: str
    goal: str
    team: list[str]
    started_at: str = field(default_factory=lambda: datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat())
    cycles: list[MobCycle] = field(default_factory=list)
    retro_notes: list[str] = field(default_factory=list)

    def start_cycle(self, driver: str, navigator: str,
                    prompt_tester: str, researcher: str) -> MobCycle:
        cycle = MobCycle(
            cycle_number=len(self.cycles) + 1,
            driver=driver,
            navigator=navigator,
            prompt_tester=prompt_tester,
            researcher=researcher,
            started_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        )
        self.cycles.append(cycle)
        return cycle

    def current_cycle(self) -> Optional[MobCycle]:
        return self.cycles[-1] if self.cycles else None

    def save(self, directory: str = "mob-logs") -> pathlib.Path:
        pathlib.Path(directory).mkdir(parents=True, exist_ok=True)
        path = pathlib.Path(directory) / f"{self.session_id}.json"
        path.write_text(json.dumps(asdict(self), indent=2))
        return path


# Example usage
if __name__ == "__main__":
    session = MobSession(
        session_id="2024-03-15-research-agent-planning-prompt",
        goal="Write and validate the planning prompt for the research agent",
        team=["alice", "bob", "carol", "dave", "eve"],
    )

    cycle = session.start_cycle(
        driver="alice",
        navigator="bob",
        prompt_tester="carol",
        researcher="dave",
    )

    cycle.add_decision("Use structured output (JSON mode) for plan steps")
    cycle.add_decision("Cap context at 4000 tokens to leave room for tool results")

    cycle.add_observation(PromptObservation(
        input_text="Summarise recent AI safety papers",
        output_text='{"steps": ["search arxiv", "filter by date", "extract abstracts"]}',
        observed_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        observer="carol",
        anomaly=None,
    ))

    cycle.add_observation(PromptObservation(
        input_text="What is 2+2?",
        output_text="4",
        observed_at=datetime.now(timezone.utc).isoformat(),
        observer="carol",
        anomaly="Agent answered directly instead of returning a structured plan — prompt constraint not enforced for trivial inputs",
    ))

    cycle.close()
    session.retro_notes.append("Anomaly: trivial inputs bypass structured output constraint — add guard in system prompt")
    path = session.save()
    print(f"Session log saved to {path}")

A team building a code review agent holds a two-hour mob session. Alice drives while Bob navigates. Carol runs the agent against a corpus of ten pull requests in a second terminal. Dave researches the model's function-calling schema documentation. During the second cycle, Carol notices the agent returns malformed JSON when a pull request diff exceeds 3000 tokens. The mob immediately adds a token-counting guard before the LLM call—a fix that would have surfaced in CI 45 minutes later under normal solo development.

Thoughtworks Engineering reports that mob programming on AI feature work reduced rework cycles on their internal document-processing agent by 40%. The team attributed the improvement to catching prompt edge cases during sessions rather than after deployment. The Prompt Tester role—an adaptation they added to the classic mob format—was cited as the highest-value change.

content: the Driver must type what the Navigator says, not what they personally think is correct. This is the core discipline of mob programming.
vague goals like 'work on the agent' produce unfocused sessions. The goal must be specific and completable within the session.
without rotation, the mob collapses into one person driving while others watch.
treating agent sessions the same as non-AI mobs misses the primary advantage of multi-observer testing.
routine non-AI code is better handled by pairing or solo work to avoid spending 5× the developer-hours.

content.text: Agree on a concrete, completable goal before the session starts.
Keep timers strict — 10 to 15 minutes per cycle, no exceptions.
Record all agent observations during the session in a structured log.
Start each session by reviewing anomalies from the previous session's log.
Use tuple, Pop, or VS Code Live Share for remote mobs with low latency.
Hold a 10-minute retrospective at the end of every session — agent behaviour surprises are learning opportunities.
Add the session log to the pull request as supporting evidence for prompt decisions.

Mob sessions are expensive per unit time but cheap per defect. A two-hour mob session with five developers costs ten developer-hours. If it prevents two production incidents caused by prompt regressions that would each take three hours to diagnose and fix, the mob session pays for itself. Mob programming scales by frequency rather than team size: small teams run one mob session per sprint per major agent feature, not daily for all work.

content: a command-line tool that manages git WIP commits and role rotation timers for mob sessions.
high-quality remote pair and mob screen-sharing tools with low latency and multi-cursor support.
enables multiple developers to edit the same file simultaneously with individual cursors.
digital whiteboards useful for the Navigator to sketch agent architecture during the session.
dedicated mob timer tools that display the role rotation countdown prominently.

content.text: Low — single observer misses non-deterministic failures
Medium — two observers, one can test while one codes
Low — reviewer sees code but not live agent output

Mob sessions should treat the agent's system prompt and tool schemas as sensitive artefacts. Do not share screens containing API keys or production credentials. Use dedicated test environments with mock data. The session log should be stored in the repository rather than in personal notes, so that prompt decisions are auditable and not lost if a team member leaves.
Mob programming adapts to agent feature work by adding Prompt Tester and Researcher roles.
Multiple simultaneous observers are the most effective counter to LLM non-determinism during development.
Role rotation every 10–15 minutes keeps all mob members engaged and prevents knowledge silos.
The session log is an essential output — it documents prompt decisions and observed anomalies.
Mob programming is expensive per hour but cheap per defect in AI feature work.
Reserve mob sessions for high-risk, high-value agent work, not all development.
